REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JUSTICE CARTER

INTRODUCTION

The parents of 13-year-old X and 11-year-old Y are unable to agree as to the living and care arrangements for their children.

The mother sought orders that the children live with her and spend time with their father on alternate weekends and for half of the school holidays.

The father sought orders that the children remain living with him. At the commencement of the final hearing the father proposed the children spend time with the mother as agreed and in default of any agreement, on alternate weekends and for half of school holiday periods. At the conclusion of the hearing, the father sought orders that reflected the minute of orders proposed by the Independent Children's Lawyer.

The Independent Children's Lawyer’s proposal was that the children remain with their father, and that the time they spend with their mother be reduced to twice each school term, for four consecutive nights in the shorter term holidays and for blocks of four nights each alternate week during the long summer holidays, as well as orders for time and communication on special occasions. In addition, the Independent Children's Lawyer sought a number of restraints on the mother’s contact and communication with the children.

The parents agree they lack the capacity to consult each other and make joint decisions for the children. Accordingly, they both seek that they have the power to make long term decisions for the children.

THE EVIDENCE

It has not been possible to include every aspect of each of the parties’ evidence. However, I have taken all the evidence into account. Just because I have not mentioned something in these reasons does not mean that I have not considered it.

Section 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) sets out that the standard of proof in these proceedings is to a balance of probabilities.

The Father

The father relied on the following documents:

(a) Amended Application for Final Orders filed on 21 January 2026;

(b) affidavit of Mr D filed on 21 January 2026;

(c) affidavit of the father filed on 22 January 2026; and

(d) affidavit of Ms E filed on 22 January 2026.

Mr D was not required by any party for cross examination. Accordingly, his affidavit was not the subject of any challenge.

The father and Ms E were required for cross examination.

I note the father’s trial affidavit refers extensively to affidavits deposed by him and filed earlier in the proceedings, with those affidavits marked as exhibits to the father’s trial affidavit. This is extremely poor drafting, and I do not regard it as compliant with the trial directions made on 7 August 2025 that required each party to file “one updated and consolidated trial Affidavit”.

Other issues with the exhibits include that many of them – such as multiple court orders and several expert reports – were entirely unnecessary. Further, the father had sought to exhibit the affidavits of other witnesses to his trial affidavit. This is clearly entirely inappropriate.

Moreover, pursuant to r 8.15(3) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (‘the Rules’) documents filed as annexures must not be accepted as evidence in the proceedings unless and until the document is separately tendered in evidence at the hearing and accepted by the Court.

The parties were advised at the commencement of the hearing that r 8.15(3) applied to the affidavits of all lay witnesses. Accordingly, save for one document tendered by the father in the usual course, none of the father’s voluminous exhibits were in evidence before me.

The Mother

The mother relied on the following documents:

(a) Amended Response filed on 31 July 2025;

(b) affidavit of the mother filed on 21 January 2026;

(c) affidavit of Mr F filed on 21 January 2026; and

(d) affidavit of Ms G filed on 21 January 2026.

The mother, Mr F and Ms G were required for cross examination.

Expert reports

The Court had the benefit of the Family Report dated 24 June 2025. The Family Report writer was required for cross examination. The father also relied on the Child Impact Report dated 27 November 2024. The writer of that report was not required for cross examination.

BACKGROUND

The father’s household

The father is 42 years old. He lives in City J, New South Wales, with his wife Ms E who is 43 years old. The father identifies as Aboriginal, with his mob being from the City H area in Victoria.

Also living at the City J property are:

Ms E’s teenage daughters, who spend alternate weeks in the care of their father; and

Mr D and Mr K. Mr D is the son of the mother in these proceedings. Mr K is the father’s eldest son. They are both 19.

Ms E’s daughters share a bedroom. An issue identified in the Child Impact Report was that X wanted her own private space. To support X’s needs, the father and Ms E have now ensured X and Y each have their own bedroom. Mr D and Mr K live in a separate self-contained unit on the property.

Unfortunately, Mr D has no relationship with his mother.

X has recently commenced Year 8 at L School. X attends from time to time upon a Dr M, who is a paediatric kidney specialist.

Y will complete Year 5 at N School this year.

Both children are doing well at school and are described positively by their teachers.

The father works full time as a tradesperson. He generally leaves home between 5.00 am and 6.00 am on weekdays and is home between 2.00 pm and 5.00 pm each weekday. He said he is generally home at about the same time as the children.

Ms E also works full time – but is able to work from home each alternate week and during all school holiday periods. On the days Ms E works at the office, she leaves home about 7.30 am taking her daughters with her to their school in City O. They return home between 5.30 pm and 6.00 pm. X leaves for school at a similar time in the mornings and catches a bus. Y leaves at about 8.30 am and rides a bike to school. The father and Ms E have set up Google alerts that remind him when it is time for him to leave.

The father, Ms E and the children in their home are all engaged in extracurricular activities. The father, Ms E and Y play football – and attend for training during the week and play on Fridays and weekends. The father also coaches Y’s football team. The children decide whether they want to watch the father play on the weekends or if they would prefer to stay home or see friends.

The father and Ms E are aware Y told the mother he was forced to play football. They said they have been very careful not to influence or pressure him after he returned to living in their home. They say it was Y’s request to return to football. I note the mother acknowledges in her affidavit that she does not believe Y does not want to play football. She described him as “very talented” and a good player.

X plays netball on Saturdays. She also goes to the gym with the father each week and they enjoy watching TV shows together.

Ms E takes her daughters to their netball games on Saturdays when they are in her care. She acknowledged that games for various family members sometimes clash, and said that she and the father tried to “share themselves” as best as they could to ensure that someone watched each of the children’s games.

Ms E and the father say all the young people living in their home get along well. Additionally, the children have friends stay for sleepovers and also enjoy sleepovers at their friends’ homes.

It is a busy household, filled with activities. It is also a home with rules and routines – in which all residents are expected to undertake allocated chores. The father and Ms E also implement boundaries around social media and mobile phone use.

It was plain from the evidence of Ms E and the father that Ms E attends to the household administration and organisation, including arranging for the children’s medical and specialist appointments. She is also the conduit for communication between the parents about the children as the father finds it anxiety provoking to communicate directly with the mother.

Ms E was an impressive witness. She was forthright, genuine and child focussed. She had reflected on the children’s comments made to the professionals and I accept her evidence that she has endeavoured to ensure the children’s needs are being met, and that she is not prioritising her children over X and Y. She was insightful regarding the pressures the children would be under and the need to be careful and cautious in discussing arrangements with the children in a way that does not make them feel like they have to “choose” one parent over another.

The mother’s household

The mother is 46 years old. She lives in Suburb P, New South Wales with her partner, Mr F who is 62 years old. The mother works in the medical field in Town Q. There was no specific evidence led as to her roster, although the mother asserted she is generally able to be available to care for the children outside school hours and can be assisted by Mr F at times as required. She deposed that she finishes work on a Friday at 4.30 pm. However, she has also been able to collect the children from their school at the conclusion of the school day each alternate Friday during school terms. This suggests she has some flexibility regarding her roster.

Mr F described himself as self-employed and said he had recently started working in transport. I do not know what hours he works which he described as “very flexible”.

It is about a two and a half to three hour drive one way from the mother’s house to the father’s house.

The mother’s home has four bedrooms. The mother’s daughter Ms G lived with the mother until late 2025. She now lives independently, in Suburb Q, about midway between the homes of the mother and father. She is 20 years old.

Regrettably, Ms G has no contact or communication with the father since she left his home in early 2021. Ms G and Mr D also have no relationship with each other.

The mother says if the children live with her, she is able to have breakfast with the children before school each day, and be home for them when they arrive home. Whilst in her care on school nights the mother said they share a family dinner and play games or watch television. The mother said the children have expressed a view that their after-school routine in their father’s home is quite exhausting. On holidays the mother said she can arrange her shifts to ensure she is more available during the day to participate in various activities with the children.

Whilst living with the mother from September 2024 to January 2025, Y participated in cricket, with training one evening and games every Saturday morning. X spent time with family friends who have horses. The mother said if X lived with her, she would likely also play netball, training one evening and playing one day on the weekend. The mother said neither she nor Mr F are engaged in sporting activities.

It appears the children’s routine is ‘less busy’ in the mother’s household. Whilst there are still rules and boundaries, the mother’s parenting style is more relaxed.

Chronology

The parents commenced a relationship in about 2008. The mother was diagnosed with cancer shortly after the parties started living together, for which she received treatment.

The children were born in 2012 and 2014, and the parents were married in 2017. They separated shortly thereafter. The father had the primary care of the children at that time and told the Family Report writer the children were able to move freely between the parental households as they remained living near each other, and they worked cooperatively. The mother told the Family Report writer that she was “threatened and battered into the corner” regarding the children living primarily with the father, and that the time she had with the children was “sparce”.

The father commenced his relationship with Ms E in early 2018. The mother commenced her relationship with Mr F in 2019.

Ms G left the mother’s care and moved to live with the father in early 2019. The father said the mother “kicked” Ms G out. The mother said she had had a “falling out” with Ms G largely as a result of the mother having less funds available for extracurricular activities following the parties’ separation.

In May 2019 the mother attempted to end her life. She was admitted to hospital and remained a voluntary inpatient for a further five days. At that time the children, Ms G and Mr D stayed with the father. He instituted parenting proceedings on 19 May 2019. The children’s time with their mother was ceased for a period, and then resumed on a supervised basis.

The mother relocated to Region S, about two and a half hours’ drive from the father’s home in about 2020.

On about 9 October 2020 the parents entered into consent orders regarding Ms G and Mr D, pursuant to which they would live with the father and spend time with the mother in accordance with their wishes. The father asserted both Ms G and Mr D elected to have no communication or contact with the mother.

The mother commenced counselling with Ms T – a mental health social worker – in October 2020. The mother deposed she attended appointments approximately monthly until August 2021.

On 13 November 2020 all parenting proceedings were finalised with further consent orders being made. Those orders provided for X and Y to live with their father and to spend time with their mother initially supervised but progressing to a regime of alternate weekends and half school holidays. The parents shared parental responsibility.

It is not in dispute that the mother availed herself of every opportunity to spend time with the children, undertaking the vast majority of the driving to do so. She said it took her almost six hours each return trip.

It is the evidence of the father and Ms E that whilst Ms G lived with them she experienced mental health difficulties. They say they endeavoured to support her as best as they could, including ensuring she received treatment and professional support.

By February 2021 the children’s time with their mother progressed to unsupervised visits.

Following the first weekend of unsupervised visits Ms G did not return to the father’s home. The mother asserted that in early 2021 Ms G had made complaints about feeling controlled and unhappy in the father’s home. According to the mother’s evidence she told the father on 5 February 2021 that Ms G did not want to live with the father and “will be living with me” commencing that day. There was no prior communication between the parents regarding this decision – which came as a surprise to the father and Ms E. Ms E said she was devastated by Ms G’s sudden and unexpected departure from their home, and in particular that Ms G and the mother had arranged this without communicating with the father or Ms E. Ms E also said that attempts to contact Ms G were unsuccessful as she switched her phone off, and subsequently changed numbers and did not provide them with that new number.

The mother says that from late 2022 X started becoming reluctant to return to the father’s care – hiding or asserting she was unwell, seeking to delay the return trip. The mother’s evidence is that from about April 2023 X became “more adamant” that she did not want to leave the mother’s home – shutting herself in her room, pretending at times to be sick, and to kick and scream, saying “I’m not going back”. However, the mother was able to ensure she returned on each occasion.

The mother wrote to the father in May 2023 and requested that for a trial period the children move to live with her and spend alternate weekends with the father. The father did not agree. It is the father’s evidence that the children’s behaviour changed after this request from the mother – which the father attributes to the children feeling pressured to choose one parent over the other. Ms E also told the school in early 2024 that the children were more defiant – attributing that behaviour to the children reporting the mother telling them they will live with her, she will be returning to court to get “papers” and that they will have their own rooms in her home.

In about late August 2024 Ms E was cleaning X’s school bag and found her diary. Ms E said she flicked through it and read negative comments about herself and that X wanted to live with the mother. She said she was devastated to read those comments made by X.

In early September 2024, the mother of a friend of X contacted Ms E and expressed concern about X’s use of Snapchat and TikTok. Ms E asked X for her phone passcode, and – without X’s agreement – Ms E commenced to monitor X’s online behaviour. Ms E said she noticed X was following what she described as “depressive pages”. X also received messages from someone on Snapchat wanting photographs of her. X had downloaded those social media apps without the father’s knowledge or consent.

Ms E raised this with the father, and they agreed that Ms E would discuss online safety issues with X. They said they felt X may be more comfortable having that conversation with Ms E rather than the father. It is common ground that neither Ms E nor the father sought to raise the issue with the mother.

On about 4 September 2024 Ms E took X to City O. Ms E said she raised the issue carefully and calmly with X, and that they had a productive discussion regarding online safety and ancillary matters. Ms E said she also raised with X that she had seen X’s diary.

On the afternoon of 4 September 2024, the mother received a phone call from X, who was distressed. X told the mother she had “locked myself in the cupboard”. The “cupboard” was actually a curated space that had been carefully set up for X as a private and comfortable space for her that she enjoyed using.

The mother said X was sobbing and whispering, saying the father and Ms E had gone through her diary, “tore strips” off her and that she was scared. X said she would run away from the father’s home and asked the mother to pick her up. The mother responded that she would get into trouble if she collected X and told her not to run away while the mother “will do something to figure things out”. The mother said Y told her that Ms E had “yelled” at him.

The father’s evidence was that after the scheduled phone call with her mother that evening, X did not appear distressed or unhappy. Ms E’s evidence was that X did not appear distressed at any point that day.

When interviewed for the Child Impact Report in November 2024 X said she got upset when Ms E looked through her diary but did not make any complaints about the conversation she had with Ms E or say that she was scared. In relation to having been asked to send inappropriate photos online, X told the report writer she was learning about technology safety and would be more mindful in the future. She also told the report writer the father never yelled at the children.

At trial, the mother said in September 2024 she went to the local court to enquire what could be done for X. She said the court advised her to seek legal advice. The mother said later that day she saw a lawyer who advised her to speak with Child Protection. At trial the mother’s evidence was she spoke with Child Protection who advised her she could collect the children if she was concerned about their welfare. In her affidavit the mother asserted that the “phone operator” at Child Protection told her given the children’s age, “they could start to decide who they want to live with”.

It is common ground that the mother did not communicate in any way with the father or Ms E regarding X’s asserted distress. Nor did the mother explore with X alternate ways in which she might support her.

In September 2024, without notice to the father, or to the children’s school, the mother attended at the school intending to remove both children and retain them in her care. She was accompanied on the journey by Mr F and Ms G.

Upon arrival, the mother told the principal she was at the school to collect the children as she believed they were not safe. The principal spoke with the children who both told her they wanted to leave with their mother.

The principal contacted the father, who subsequently arrived at the school. The police were also called to the school.

After speaking with the father, the principal informed the mother that the children were going to leave with the father, and she would not stop him. The mother left the principal’s office and ran towards the children, calling out to them. The school principal completed an incident report in which she described:

[r]aised voices, arguing, and physical aggression increased as they fought over the children....[the father] had a hold of [X] against his chest with one arm around her head pinning her against him while [the mother] was trying to pull her free. The principal intervened directing the children back inside the school grounds and when [the mother and father] followed still arguing, the principal instructed them to leave the school. Both [the mother and father] were insisting that the children go home with them...

The father acknowledged he was holding X’s arm quite firmly, which he characterised as reflexive rather than aggressive. He let go when X said he was hurting her. The mother said the children were visibly upset and crying. She said she told the children “you need to tell dad what you want” and X said “I want to live with mum”. The mother said she then:

...placed my hand on [the father’s] chest to hold him at arm’s length and told him “the Children have told you what they want, and they want to live with me, they are old enough to decide ...you need to listen to them”

The mother said the father then tried to “stand over [her] in an intimidating manner”, and she told him “you don’t scare me anymore”.

This occurred in the children’s presence. The children were then taken back to the principal’s office.

The police arrived and spoke with the children. They said they “did not have jurisdiction” and allowed the mother to take the children from the school. According to the principal’s incident report, when the police were unable to assist the parents to reach an agreement, they called their supervisor and somewhat appallingly;

[it] was decided to bring both parents into the foyer, and bring the children out to the foyer as well and whoever the children went to would be the parent taking the children home from school. [X] and [Y] both chose [the mother].

It is difficult to imagine a more stressful and distressing situation for the children – forced in front of their school principal, police and their parents to “choose” with whom they would leave.

It is the mother’s evidence that on the way home X was visibly distraught. The mother deposed that she told the children she was “very proud of them” and that they were “strong” to tell the principal and the police “what they wanted”.

The mother then retained the children in her care until January 2025. In that time, the mother:

enrolled the children in a new school – U School – near her home and took them to see another prospective school near her home;

obtained a prescription for Y for melatonin which she gave to both children. She said this was necessary as the children were experiencing difficulties sleeping;

obtained a prescription for X for Amitriptyline, which the mother said was to manage headaches and night terrors she said X was experiencing;

did not arrange any communication between the children and their father until 8 October 2024, about four weeks after they were abruptly removed from his care and after orders were made; and

did not facilitate any face-to-face time until that was arranged by the Independent Children's Lawyer over Christmas, approaching four months after they were removed from the father’s care.

The mother demonstrated limited insight into how problematic her actions were. This is a matter that will be considered later in these reasons.

It is the mother’s evidence that the children settled well into their new school.

It is also the mother’s evidence that a Dr V prescribed melatonin to Y only but told the mother she could also give it to X. She said Dr V told her to start the children on a low dose – of 1mg – and to increase the dose “if required”. It was also the mother’s evidence that she purchased an over-the-counter bottle of melatonin for X which had 10mg doses.

The father issued proceedings on 9 September 2024.

The mother took the children on a tour of W School on about 19 or 20 September 2024.

The mother arranged for the children to attend upon a psychologist, which commenced in September 2024.

At the return of the parenting proceedings on 3 October 2024 interim orders were made for the children to remain with their mother and for time and communication with the father to commence. An Independent Children's Lawyer was appointed, and a Child Impact Report was ordered.

The mother re-engaged with Ms T in about October 2024. She attended approximately monthly until September 2025 and has resumed monthly attendances this year.

On 15 November 2024 the mother asked Ms E about the tickets she had arranged for X to attend the end of year celebrations at N School to be held on [...] December 2024. In the email exchange Ms E explained they had sold their tickets. That was understandable as X had been removed from N School by the mother and enrolled at U School.

On 19 November 2024 the mother requested the father post a dress X had left at the father’s home which she had intended to wear at the end of year celebrations at N School. Ms E located the dress and posted it on 27 November 2024.

The mother said the dress did not arrive in time for X to wear it at the end of year celebration at U School – although the mother failed to identify the date of that celebration. According to an email sent by the mother’s solicitors to the other parties on Thursday 28 November 2024 there was reference to the formal taking place “this coming Saturday night” which would have been 30 November 2024. The tracking information shows the dress was available for collection at the post office in Lansdowne on 2 December 2024. Ms E said she was not told there was any urgency in locating and sending the dress.

The last day of school at N School was [...] December 2024. The mother drove X to N School so she could spend the day there and farewell her friends and teachers. She took Y as well. Ms E and the paternal grandparents also attended the festivities at school that day for a short period.

On 23 January 2025 following a contested hearing, the court made further interim orders, returning the children to the father’s primary care. Orders were made for the children to spend alternate weekends with their mother and time on holidays. Changeovers not at school were to occur at Town C – about half way between the parents’ homes.

It is common ground that the mother did not explicitly advise the father when the children returned to his care at the end of January 2025 that they were both taking melatonin and that X was taking Amitriptyline. The mother deposes only to having sent a message to the father in late December 2024 advising – incorrectly – that both children had been prescribed melatonin. There is no evidence the mother informed the father regarding X’s prescription for Amitriptyline. She said she did not directly tell the father about the medication when the children were returned to the father’s home, asserting the situation was “very volatile” and “distressing” for her and the children and she did not feel able to contact the father about anything, including the medication. She also did not instruct her lawyers to write to the father regarding medication.

When the children were returned to the father’s care at the end of January 2025 it is apparent that the father and Ms E were aware the children were taking melatonin. Ms E spoke with the children’s general practitioner on 24 January 2025 regarding the children’s use of melatonin for sleep. The general practitioner strongly advised against medication for sleep unless that had been prescribed by a paediatrician.

On about 29 January 2025, Ms E found the Amitriptyline in X’s room. That had been provided to X when she left the mother’s home with instructions from the mother that X inform the father about the medication. The mother acknowledged she had no way to know if the information regarding the medication was passed on to the father at all, or accurately. She surprisingly suggested that requiring the children to pass on that information was teaching them “responsibility”.

Ms E took X to see another general practitioner the following day. The advice was to reduce X’s dosage and then cease it. A letter was written to X’s paediatrician, but I do not have any evidence as to the paediatrician’s response.

That X was taking Amitriptyline without adult oversight is particularly problematic as her dosage required the tablet to be halved. The father does not know whether X took the medication as prescribed when she first returned to his care and before the medication was discovered. I note the mother deposed that she kept the Amitriptyline away from the children at her home and that it was administered under the supervision of herself, Mr F or Ms G. It is difficult to understand the mother’s poor communication with the father regarding the medication – or what the children made of the situation where they were supervised taking the medication in their mother’s home but left to attend to that by themselves in their father’s home.

The children were weaned off their medications in accordance with medical advice and are now medication free in the father’s home. The father and Ms E say the children have not experienced difficulties sleeping and are progressing well. According to medical records, X denies feeling upset or down, and said she was sleeping well. The mother also does not assert that X continues to suffer from headaches that require medication.

There was an incident on 19 April 2025 when the mother’s car broke down in Town Z. Mr F was frustrated and angry that Ms E did not agree to drive further to collect the children, and he expressed that anger at Ms E when changeover occurred that afternoon, including calling her a “fucking bitch”. The children likely heard Mr F speak to Ms E in that derogatory manner as they were present and out of the car at the time.

It is the mother’s evidence that since returning to live with the father, X continues to seek to delay returning to the father’s home, claiming she is suffering a headache, and that Y has also expressed reluctance to return. She said he becomes withdrawn when the time to return approaches. She said Y has asked her how old he has to be before he can choose where he lives. She said since January 2025, Y has refused to leave the car until she reassures him he will be returning to her care shortly. However, there is no evidence that changeovers have been delayed or problematic.

In addition, neither parent reported any significant incident of the children being upset, or distressed after returning to their father’s care, save that the children complained at Christmas in 2025 that they were left alone for about 5 hours. That is denied by the father and Ms E who said they celebrated their family Christmas on 21 December 2025. They said on Christmas Day they went to the gym for an hour and were otherwise at home.

The father and Ms E describe that the children have settled back into their home, school lives and community.

The Family Consultant

The family participated in a Family Report that was published on 24 June 2025. In that report, the writer supported the children living with their mother and spending alternate weekends with their father. The report writer considered that given the ages of the children, weight should be given to their views.

At trial, however, the Family Report writer expressed concern that the children may well be experiencing pressure and loyalty binds – such that the court should be cautious about placing substantial weight on the children’s views.

HOW ARE THE CHILDREN’S BEST INTERESTS ASSESSED?

Section 60CA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) sets out that when deciding whether to make a particular parenting order in relation to children, the best interests of the children must be my paramount consideration.

The objects of Part VII of the Act inform how I must exercise my discretion. Those objects include ensuring that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety (s 60B(a)). The legislation further provides that I must also give effect to the 1989 Convention on the Rights of the Child (s 60B(b)).

THE GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS

There are a range of considerations set out in ss 60CC(2) and (2A) of the Act that I must take into account in determining what is in the child’s best interests.

Section 60CC(2) sets out a non-hierarchical list of considerations which I must take into account being:

(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:

(i) the child; and

(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);

(b) any views expressed by the child;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

Subsection 60CC(2A) provides:

In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a), the court must include consideration of:

(a) any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child); and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.

The father and the children identify as Aboriginal. Accordingly, I must also consider the provisions of subsection 60CC(3) being:

(a) the child’s right to enjoy the child’s Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander culture, by having the support, opportunity and encouragement necessary:

(i) to connect with, and maintain their connection with, members of their family and with their community, culture, country and language; and

(ii) to explore the full extent of that culture, consistent with the child’s age and developmental level and the child’s views; and

(iii) to develop a positive appreciation of that culture; and

(b) the likely impact any proposed parenting order under this Part will have on that right.

Promoting safety

Physical safety

Whilst both parents made complaints about the safety of the children in the care of the other, I am not satisfied the children are at risk of physical harm in the care of either parent. The single incident in which Mr F picked Y up when he was misbehaving did not hurt or harm him.

The children’s complaints to the mother

The mother relied on a number of complaints she says the children made to her about their treatment in the father’s household. Those complaints were not raised by the children with either the Child Impact Report writer or the Family Report writer, nor were they reported to the children’s counsellors. Where the children did speak directly to court experts, their accounts were materially different from the mother’s reports. In particular, X told the Child Impact Report writer that the father did not yell at the children, that the chores required of her were reasonable, and that she did not feel frightened. Although she described Ms E as sometimes “mean” when chores were not completed, she said this was annoying rather than distressing.

Allegations that items were discarded, clothing restricted, toys removed, or that the children were excluded from Aboriginal cultural activities were not raised by the children with the court child experts and were not supported by independent evidence. I am not satisfied those complaints accurately reflect the children’s experiences.

I accept the evidence of Ms E that she has made sustained efforts to ensure all children in the household are treated equitably, with age‑appropriate chores and expectations, and that she has reflected upon and responded to the children’s perceptions about fairness and inclusion.

In these circumstances, I am satisfied that a number of the complaints relied upon by the mother were exaggerated or framed in a way that reflected the children’s desire to appease her. I also accept that the mother may, unintentionally, have interpreted the children’s comments through the lens of her own past experiences with the father – interpreting the children’s comments in an adverse way. These matters are relevant to the assessment of the weight to be given to the children’s views.

Evidence of the adult children

It is regrettable that both parents adduced evidence from the adult children. I am satisfied that Ms G and Mr D have been significantly exposed to parental conflict.

In her evidence, Ms G described living in the father’s household as ‘not a positive experience’. She deposed she was the person in the household primarily responsible for caring for the children when the father and Ms E were absent, including getting them to and from school, feeding them and doing homework with them. In her oral evidence Ms G agreed it was not inappropriate that she was expected to assist in this way.

Mr D’s evidence was that the father provided safe, supportive and appropriate care to all the children. He corroborated there were fewer rules, boundaries and expectations in the mother’s home. Sadly, it appears that relationship is completely broken down.

I put limited weight on Ms G’s evidence insofar as it related to X and Y being “much happier” in their mother’s care. Ms G has not lived with the children in the father’s home for several years, and her experiences, whilst genuinely felt, are not the same as those of the younger children. I also accept that both Ms G, and the father and Ms E, experienced the time before Ms G left their care as distressing as her mental health declined, and that their perspectives are not mutually exclusive. I also accept there were likely better ways in which Ms G could have been supported.

I put little weight on Mr D’s complaints about the care he received in his mother’s home given his age at the time.

Allegations of breach of privacy

I am not satisfied that the children’s privacy is breached in the father’s household. Although it was unfortunate that Ms E read parts of X’s diary, it was inadvertently discovered. Monitoring X’s phone and social media use was a protective and appropriate response to concerns about online grooming. The limits placed on technology use were developmentally appropriate.

Whilst Ms E had an appropriate discussion with X about online safety, it is regrettable that neither she nor the father felt able to raise the issue with the mother. Accordingly, the mother had no context when X complained to her that the father and Ms E had accessed her phone.

I accept the evidence of the father and Ms E that they had a Google Home device set up with reminders, alarms and notifications, but that the camera on the device is not used. This was corroborated by Mr D.

Dietary complaints

Complaints about diet, weight, and food practices in each household were not raised with the court experts and were unsupported by medical evidence. I therefore make no findings about these matters.

The incident on 19 April 2025

Mr F’s conduct at changeover on 19 April 2025 was inappropriate and exposed the children to adult conflict. I accept, however, that this was an isolated incident.

Conclusion as to safety concerns

Taking all of the evidence into account, I am satisfied that neither parent presents an unacceptable risk to the children. The household rules, routines and expectations in the father’s home are age‑appropriate and do not adversely affect the children’s wellbeing. The most significant risk to the children’s psychological safety arises not from either household, but from their exposure to ongoing parental conflict.

Views expressed by the children

The children have on multiple occasions expressed a wish to live with their mother. Having regard to their ages, those views warrant consideration. However, a central issue is the weight to be afforded to them in light of all the evidence.

At the conclusion of the hearing, I was satisfied that limited weight should be given to the children’s expressed views. The reasons advanced by the children in favour of their mother’s home were, in large part, focussed on comparatively superficial matters, including a less structured household, fewer chores, greater access to mobile phones, and lifestyle preferences. In X’s case, her expressed preference was also influenced by the belief that she would have her own room and be able to have a horse if she lived with her mother.

I accept the evidence of the Family Report writer that these children have been embroiled in prolonged litigation and have been aware for some time of their mother’s desire to change the living arrangements. I also accept that the children may have been influenced – consciously or otherwise – by a perception that their mother is more emotionally vulnerable than their father. In those circumstances, it appears the children have been caught in a loyalty bind and may have aligned their views with what they perceive would please their mother. The Family Report writer also accepted that the children did not appear to appreciate the consequences of a decision to change residence, and that X’s continued use of comfort items suggested a degree of emotional immaturity. She agreed that these factors mitigated against significant weight being given to the children’s expressed views.

Y’s presentation to the Child Impact Report writer illustrated the pressure he felt to choose between his parents. Although he expressed a preference to live with his mother, he did not report feeling unsafe in his father’s care, regarded the allocation of chores as fair, and became visibly distressed when discussing the parental conflict. I accept the Independent Children’s Lawyer’s submission that Y appears to be struggling to navigate the dispute without causing emotional harm to either parent.

Consistently with what I have already observed, the arrangements for the children’s older siblings may also have signposted to the children that there is a binary choice to be made between the parents, reinforcing the pressure they experience.

X’s complaints about her father’s household, when examined closely, were limited to age‑appropriate chores, differing rules about mobile phone use, and relational difficulties with her step‑siblings. She did not report being fearful of her father or Ms E. Allegations of bullying or preferential treatment lacked specificity and were not corroborated by any concrete examples or independent evidence. I am satisfied that these complaints were either exaggerated or reflective of ordinary tensions in a blended household. I also note that in relation to the bullying claims, X’s concerns appeared to be primarily school-based rather than arising from the care arrangements in the father's home.

Taking these matters together, while the children’s views have been carefully considered, they do not provide a reliable or determinative guide to what arrangements will promote their long-term welfare.

Developmental, psychological, emotional, and cultural needs of the children

Both children expressed a view to the Family Report writer that they had little one on one time with their father, and that they feel they lack meaningful engagement with him. This is contrary to their experience in the mother’s home, where she plays games with them and engages with them and their lives at a deeper level. The mother also appears to provide more physical affection to the children.

Currently the father spends time with X generally once a week together at the gym and he coaches Y’s football team. Whilst those activities are important and provide the opportunity for the father and children to build a close connection, the father is encouraged to find other ways to meaningfully connect with the children and this cannot be outsourced to Ms E.

As observed in the Child Impact Report, as X enters adolescence and puberty it is not surprising there is a gender affinity to the mother.

Capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the children to provide for their developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs

It is clear that each parent is able to meet many of the children’s needs. However, each also demonstrates shortcomings which are relevant to an assessment of capacity.

The father

The father’s work commitments and sporting involvement limit his availability to spend one on one time with the children. Nevertheless, the Family Report writer observed a positive bond between the father and the children and described him as attentive and responsive.

I accept that the father seeks to protect the children from adult conflict and from feeling pressure to align with either parent. However, his conflict‑avoidant approach means he can struggle to engage with the children about emotionally complex matters, which may cause them to perceive him as dismissive or disinterested. While shielding the children from conflict is appropriate, the father would benefit from greater emotional engagement with them.

I am satisfied that the parenting strategies, routines and boundaries in the father’s household are age‑appropriate, protective and reasonable. However, it reflects poorly on both the father and Ms E that concerns about online grooming were not raised with the mother, demonstrating ongoing difficulties in parental communication even where the children’s safety may be engaged.

The mother

The mother has a history of mental health difficulties. However, there is no evidence that her mental health currently impairs her capacity to provide safe and appropriate care, and the Family Report writer did not identify her mental health as posing a risk to the children.

I accept that the mother has a close and loving relationship with the children and, in many respects, is more emotionally attuned to them. She provides focussed individual time, engages in shared activities, and supports X’s interests.

Notwithstanding those strengths, I have significant concerns about the mother’s insight and judgment. In particular, her failure to correct X’s belief that she would be able to have a horse if she lived with her raises concerns about emotional attunement.

More significantly, the mother’s unilateral removal of the children in September 2024, her failure to facilitate communication or face‑to‑face time with the father for an extended period thereafter, and her ongoing view that the children should determine where they live and with whom they have a relationship, demonstrate a lack of insight into the children’s developmental needs and the importance of supporting their relationship with both parents.

Despite now having context to X’s statements, the mother maintained at trial she was acting protectively and would attend the school again as she did in September 2024.

There was no reason advanced by the mother as to the delay in the resumption of time and communication, other than the children did not want to speak with or see their father and the decision was left to them. No genuine steps were taken by her or in her household to promote the father/child relationships after the children were removed from their father’s care. That reflects poorly on the mother and raises concern about her ability to ensure the children’s relationships with their father would be maintained and supported if they were residing in her primary care.

I accept the concern expressed by the Independent Children’s Lawyer that exposing the children to a sense of entitlement to determine arrangements has placed them under pressure and has been particularly distressing for Y.

It is essential that the mother ensures the children understand that court orders must be complied with, that care arrangements are not a matter for the children to determine, and that she does not, implicitly or explicitly, encourage agitation for change. Failure to do so risks perpetuating the emotional burden the children already experience.

The benefit to the children of being able to have a relationship with their parents, and other people who are significant to the child

The children will benefit from having a meaningful relationship with both of their parents, their step‑parents, and their siblings and step‑siblings.

The evidence demonstrates a concerning pattern in relation to the preservation of relationships with a non‑residential parent. It was a striking feature of the evidence that Mr D is estranged from the mother, Ms G is estranged from the father, and Mr D and Ms G have no relationship with each other. I am satisfied that these fractured relationships are, at least in part, the product of unresolved parental conflict and a failure by both parents to actively support and encourage the maintenance of those relationships. I also note the limited facilitation of the father/child relationship for an extended period when the children came into the mother’s care.

These matters raise concern about the risk that, if the children were to live with their mother, their relationship with their father and paternal family members may not be adequately supported or preserved.

By contrast, while the children have lived with their father, they have maintained a close and loving relationship with their mother. I am less confident that the converse would occur if the children were to live primarily with the mother.

The history or family violence, abuse or neglect, and any family violence order that applies or has applied

It is the mother’s assertion that during the relationship she was subjected to emotional and psychological abuse, and coercive controlling behaviours by the father. That was denied by the father, who asserted the mother perpetrated those behaviours on him. Given the manner in which the parties ran their respective cases, it is not necessary to resolve these factual disputes. This is particularly so in circumstances where:

(a) neither parent has sought a family violence protection order against the other at any time;

(b) the police were never involved in any dispute between the parents; and

(c) the parents agree that there has been no violence between them post separation.

I have already referred to the incident at changeover on 19 April 2025.

The child’s right to enjoy the child’s Aboriginal culture and the impact of orders on those rights

The father identifies as Aboriginal, as do the children. There was limited evidence that the father proactively facilitates the children’s connection to culture. I formed the impression the father had been somewhat cut off from his heritage – and there were limited opportunities for him to explore his indigenous connections when he was younger. He told the Family Report writer he is now gaining a greater appreciation of his indigenous heritage through his grandparents. Should the children remain living with the father they will presumably have greater access to their paternal great grandparents and a greater opportunity to understand their cultural connections.

At N School there is a Koori choir – in which X participated. She also participated in the school’s Aboriginal netball team. I do not know what activities Y is engaged with as part of that school program for Aboriginal children. I do not know what program is available to X at her high school.

The mother deposed that when the children were in her care she tried to find activities that would allow them to connect with their Aboriginal background. She did not identify any activities or groups that the children participated in with community, asserting that many activities were closed over the Christmas break. They came into her care in the first week of September.

The mother refers to having the children “do aboriginal style dot pointing [sic]” and encouraging the children “to participate in aboriginal work and days learning about the culture”. She did not identify whether that is something she is endeavouring the teach the children, or whether she has connected the children with community for those cultural activities beyond saying she had emailed the Indigenous Liaison Officer at her work about a month before the final hearing to request he spend some time with the children teaching them about their culture and heritage. He had not responded to that email.

The mother acknowledged that there was no Aboriginal program at the primary school the children previously attended when they were in her care. She has suggested the children would be enrolled at W School if they lived with her. She said that school has an Indigenous choir. I understand that is a fee-paying school and there was no evidence adduced as to how the mother proposed to fund those fees.

PARENTING ORDERS TO BE MADE

Decision making

Each of the parents seeks sole decision-making responsibility.

It is readily apparent there is no coparenting relationship between the parents and they are unable to liaise with each other and reach agreement regarding important issues concerning the children’s long-term care. In particular the parents have different attitudes towards medical treatments and interventions for the children. They also propose different schools. I agree with the observations of the Family Report writer that shared decision making would require significant improvements in the co-parenting relationship, particularly as to their ability to communicate and share information.

Counsel for the Independent Children's Lawyer readily conceded the decision making responsibility could be confined to medical and educational issues and did not need to be broader. I also note that the father indicated he did not intend to change the children’s schools, or medical practitioners.

Live with

For the reasons already outlined, I am satisfied that the children’s expressed views should be treated with caution. It is highly likely that the children have experienced pressure to align with their mother. I have found that their complaints about the father’s household are relatively minor, and that notwithstanding X’s entry into adolescence, she remains emotionally vulnerable.

I am concerned that a further change of residence would be disruptive to the children’s stability. The children have already experienced significant disruption to their schooling in 2024. A change in residence would require further removal from their schools, extracurricular activities and established peer relationships, with X potentially facing a fourth change of school within a relatively short period.

The children appear to be progressing well in their father’s care. Since returning to his home, the children have not required medication for sleep difficulties, and X has not required medical treatment for headaches. Neither child raised ongoing concerns about sleep or health with the court child experts.

I am also concerned that a change of residence would pose a real risk to the children’s relationship with their father and with members of his household, including their siblings. By contrast, while living with their father, the children have been able to maintain a close and loving relationship with their mother.

Taking these matters together, I am not satisfied that a change to the children’s living arrangements would promote their best interests.

Time with

At the conclusion of the hearing, the Independent Children’s Lawyer proposed a reduction in the children’s time arrangements, principally due to concerns that, if the children were to live with the mother, there was a real risk that their relationship with the father would not be preserved.

Those concerns were informed by the history of fractured relationships within the family, including the absence of a relationship between Ms G and the father, the delay in the resumption of the children’s communication and time with the father after they were removed, and the limited facilitation of the father/child relationship during that period.

I accept the evidence of the Family Report writer that the children have been significantly affected by prolonged litigation, and that awareness of the proceedings may exacerbate feelings of uncertainty and pressure. Where children perceive a parent to be emotionally vulnerable, they may align their views to please that parent. In that context, it was suggested that reducing the frequency or duration of time might alleviate the pressure the children experience.

However, I am not satisfied that a reduction in the children’s time would promote their best interests. Both children have expressed a wish for more time with their mother, and I am concerned that a reduction in time may be experienced by them as punitive or as a failure to listen to their expressed wishes.

In particular, having regard to X’s age and stage of development, and the evidence that she may have a gender affinity with her mother, a reduction in time may be counterproductive and increase her reluctance to return after time, or contribute to more withdrawn or defiant behaviours in the father’s household.

The longest period of stability for the children occurred between the orders being made in 2020 and the events of September 2024, during which the current time arrangements operated. During those years the children’s care arrangements were predictable and consistent. Whilst the mother asserted the children were reluctant to return to the father’s home, they did so on each occasion. Those arrangements have substantially been reinstated and have been functioning for the past 12 months, without significant interruption or non-compliance. I note the mother’s own evidence at trial was there had been “one incident” over the last few years (referring to her removal of the children from their school) and that “everything else has run smoothly”.

I accept the Family Report writer’s observation that the children require safety, stability, routine and consistency in their relationships and care. I am not persuaded that reducing the children’s time with their mother would enhance those protective factors.

In relation to special occasions, it is appropriate that the children spend Mother’s Day and Father’s Day with the relevant parent for the full weekend, given the distances involved.

As to the long summer holidays, I am satisfied that a week‑about arrangement best balances the children’s need for stability with their relationships with each parent. Making prescriptive orders for particular days, including Christmas Eve or Christmas Day, risks unnecessary travel and disruption. It will be a matter for the parents to reach agreement as to those days within the framework of a week‑about arrangement.

Changeover

Currently, changeovers that do not occur at school occur at Town C petrol station. That means generally the mother drives the whole way to collect the children at the commencement of their time with her, and then the parties meet halfway at the conclusion of time.

The Independent Children's Lawyer’s proposed orders sought that changeovers should occur as agreed in writing and otherwise at Town C. The father’s amended application sought a continuation of the current order.

The mother’s proposal – on the basis that the children live with her – was for the father to collect the children from school at the start of his time with the children and changeover on the Sunday would be at Town C.

I am satisfied that the current arrangement – whereby the mother collects the children from school at the commencement of time where changeovers occur on the last day of term – should continue. All changeovers that occur outside school hours shall occur at Town C.

Happily this is a matter in which the parents agree the children do not find the travel onerous.

Travel

No evidence was adduced in relation to overseas travel. In the absence of any evidence in this regard I do not intend to make orders as sought by the father and the Independent Children's Lawyer for the obtaining of passports or for travel outside the Commonwealth of Australia.

Communication

The children have had liberal communication with their mother when in their father’s care.

However, I am concerned that the frequency of communication between X and her mother places X under strain. The Family Report records the mother saying she speaks with the children every day and that “[X] expressed her disappointment every day, which is difficult.” Daily communication appears to create an ongoing sense of obligation for X to manage her mother’s emotions, which is an inappropriate burden for a child. Ms E’s evidence was that following communication with their mother, the children’s defiance increased or they become more withdrawn.

I also agree with the observations of the Independent Children's Lawyer that daily communication may be disruptive to the children’s time with their father. She suggested a more structured and limited arrangement.

Introducing firmer boundaries around telephone contact would help to alleviate any sense of pressure. Reducing calls to once a week, rather than daily, would give X more emotional space, lessen the expectation that she provide constant updates about her feelings, and support a healthier and more sustainable pattern of communication between X and her mother.

The proposal made by the Independent Children's Lawyer for additional calls on special occasions is also appropriate.

Communication between parents

The parents shall communicate via text message or WhatsApp group chat. Each partner can participate in those chats. This reflects the current arrangements which have enabled communication to occur, albeit limited, to date.

The Independent Children's Lawyer’s proposals for keeping each other informed about medical emergencies, and contact numbers and addresses are also clearly sensible and appropriate orders.

Injunctions

The Independent Children's Lawyer sought a number of restraints. I am not minded to make some of those injunctions. First, I note the proposal the mother be restrained from removing the children from the care of the father, and from having any communication with the children save as set out in my orders. Other than the removal of the children in September 2024, the mother has been substantially compliant with orders of the court. It is troubling that the mother indicated she would act similarly if the circumstances were repeated. On balance, however, and given the substantial history of compliance, I am not of the view such injunctions are currently necessary.

I am also not of the view it is necessary to require the mother to notify the father and consider his response before she makes any reports to Child Protection or police in the event either child makes a disclosure to her. On most occasions, that would be an entirely appropriate course of action. However, there may also be circumstances where that would be entirely inappropriate.

I am concerned the children have been somewhat caught up in the adult conflict. I am of the view that the restraints on each parent from discussing the proceedings with the children, providing the children with any documents filed in these proceedings, and from denigrating the other parent or member of their family in the presence of the children are appropriate and protective. I am also of the view that it is appropriate that the adults communicate messages with each other – rather than requiring the children to be involved. An order to that effect will require the parents to liaise with each other about matters such as medication, rather than leaving that up to the children to communicate. In relation to these matters, I am of the view that the restraints as proposed by the Independent Children's Lawyer are appropriate.

Costs

I do not propose to make any orders that the parents pay the costs of the Independent Children's Lawyer. I had little evidence as to the financial circumstances of each parent – but it is not in dispute that each parent earns a modest income. Pursuant to my orders the parents will each need to meet the children’s needs when they are in his or her care, and any order that requires a parent to contribute to the costs of the Independent Children's Lawyer will likely impinge on the party’s ability to support the children.

In all the circumstances I am dismissing the application for costs made by the Independent Children's Lawyer.

For all of the foregoing reasons, I make the orders as are set out.
